title: Private Equity Healthcare Deals: 2025 in Review
content:
[Skip to content](#main)


[Private Equity Stakeholder Project PESP](/)

[Donate here](https://pestakeholder.org/take-action/fund-pesps-work/)

[Reports](https://pestakeholder.org/explore-the-issues/?pesp_type=40)

# Private Equity Healthcare Deals: 2025 in Review

February 11, 2026

### **Overview**

In light of private equity’s continued interest in healthcare and the risks associated with private equity ownership of healthcare companies, the Private Equity Stakeholder Project (PESP) [tracks private equity-backed healthcare deals](https://pestakeholder.org/private-equity-healthcare-acquisitions/) each month and produces an annual summary exploring trends in private equity deal activity. This report looks at the 2025 private equity deals data in review, identifying and analyzing recent investment trends in healthcare.

Click [here](https://pestakeholder.org/private-equity-healthcare-acquisitions/) to view a catalog of our monthly acquisitions tracking for 2025 and previous annual reports.

**Jump ahead:**

* [Summary of data](#summary)
* [Case studies](#case)
  + [Health IT](#it)
  + [Dental care](#dental)
  + [Outpatient care](#outpatient)
  + [Home health and hospice](#hospice)
  + [Pharma services](#pharma)
* [Joint ventures](#jv)
* [Methodology](#methodology)

### Summary of data

* In 2025, PESP tracked 1029 private equity-backed leveraged buyouts (151), add-on acquisitions (664), and growth investments (214), involving 420 platform companies and 708 investment firms.[[1]](#_ftn1)
  + For explanations of leveraged buyouts, add-on acquisitions, and growth investments, click [here](https://pestakeholder.org/private-equity-healthcare-acquisitions/).
* Globally, private equity dealmaking in healthcare saw a [resurgence and record growth](https://www.bain.com/insights/healthcare-private-equity-market-2025-global-healthcare-private-equity-report-2026/) in 2025, according to research from Bain & Company. Deal value was the highest on record, and deal volume was the second highest on record. In North America, deal volume was essentially even between 2024 and 2025, a finding reflected in the U.S. specific data that PESP tracked.[[2]](#_ftn2) Even in [the current higher interest rate and high tariff](https://www.bain.com/insights/healthcare-private-equity-market-2025-global-healthcare-private-equity-report-2026/) environment, private equity dealmaking is remaining steady in the U.S..
* Health IT (151 deals), dental care (149), outpatient care (148), medtech (117), and behavioral health (56) were the busiest subsectors among those PESP tracked.
* In addition to buyouts, add-on acquisitions, and growth investments, private equity firms are increasingly using joint ventures with nonprofit health systems as a growth strategy that can provide them with trusted brands and access to geographic markets they might otherwise not readily access.
* In light of continued investor interest in healthcare and the risks associated with private equity ownership of healthcare companies, including “stealth consolidation” that can lead to higher prices, state and federal policymakers should update laws and regulations to increase oversight of healthcare mergers and acquisitions.

Explore the following tables and graphs for an overview of private equity healthcare dealmaking in 2025.

### Case studies

The following case studies explore the three busiest categories PESP tracked (Health IT, dental care, and outpatient care) and two other categories PESP has chosen to highlight: home health/hospice and pharma services. PESP periodically covers various health subsectors in greater depth in our monthly acquisitions blog posts and in long-form reports. To read more about categories that are not covered here, you can visit an [archive of our past work](https://pestakeholder.org/pesp-healthcare-archive/) or our [acquisitions landing page](https://pestakeholder.org/private-equity-healthcare-acquisitions/).

#### Health IT

Health Information Technology (IT) was the busiest healthcare category in terms of private equity deal activity in 2025 that PESP tracked (151 deals), and the second busiest category tracked in 2024 (140 deals). This broad category encompasses a variety of information technology companies, including practice [management software, electronic health record (EHR) software, and revenue cycle management companies.](https://pestakeholder.org/news/private-equity-health-care-acquisitions-january-2023/)

[Recent](https://cepr.net/publications/financialization-through-health-it-part-1/)[research](https://cepr.net/publications/healthcare-financialization-through-information-technology-part-2/) from the Center for Economic Policy and Research (CEPR) argues that federal laws (like the 2009 HITECH Act) and other regulations that intended to modernize healthcare infrastructure and control costs have had the unintended consequences of fueling the growth of a massive Health IT industry. This industry has become increasingly financialized

title: Healthcare Private Equity Market 2025: Resurgence and Record Growth
content:
# Healthcare Private Equity Market 2025: Resurgence and Record Growth

**Source:** Bain & Company — 2026 Global Healthcare Private Equity Report
**Authors:** Nirad Jain, Kara Murphy, Dmitry Podpolny, Dieter Meyer, Vikram Kapur, Alex Boulton

---

## 🏆 Record-Breaking Headline Numbers

| Metric | 2025 Estimate | Comparison |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| **Disclosed Deal Value** | **$191 billion** | Surpassed previous record set in 2021 |
| **Buyout Volume** | **~445 deals** | Second-highest annual total (behind 2021) |
| **Exit Value** | **$156 billion** | Second-highest ever; up from $54B in 2024 |
| **Deals >$1B (exits)** | **40+ deals** | Up from just 16 in 2024 |
| **Exit Volume** | Third all-time | — |

> "Healthcare private equity delivered a record performance in 2025, with disclosed deal value exceeding an estimated $191 billion, surpassing the previous high in 2021."

---

## 📈 Key Drivers

- **High levels of dry powder** to deploy
- **Growing cohort of sponsor-owned assets** reaching end of fund lives
- Both trends signal continued tailwinds and set the stage for an active 2026

---

## 🌍 Regional Performance

### Europe — Fueled by Biopharma & Provider
- Deal value **doubled to ~$59 billion**
- Top 5 deals (all biopharma) accounted for **65% of total deal value**
- **15 deals exceeded $1 billion** (vs. 3 in 2023, 4 in 2024) — reemergence of large-cap activity
- Exit value rose to an estimated **$53 billion** after a steep 2024 drop
- **Notable:** Bain Capital/Cinven's sale of STADA Arzneimittel AG to CapVest-led group

### North America — Propelled by Large Transactions
- Q2 pullback: deal count **-19%**, value **-37%** vs. Q1 (policy shifts, trade tensions, tariff uncertainty)
- **26 deals exceeded $1 billion** through November (vs. 14 in full-year 2024); **>70% were sponsor-to-sponsor**
- Exit activity rose to expected **$90 billion** (vs. $35 billion in 2024)
- Total volumes slightly up vs. 2024 but below 2021 highs

### Asia-Pacific — Broad Strength Across Markets
- Deal value **set a record**, exceeding 2021 high by **>30%**
- Provider/hospital deal value expected to **double** from $5B (2024) to **>$10B** (2025)
- Exit value rose **>20%**, volume up **~6%**
- **Greater China more than doubled** 2024 performance in volume and value (driven by biopharma and medtech)
- Japan, India, Australia/New Zealand also saw notable growth

---

## 🔬 Sector Performance

### Biopharma — Commanding Large Share of Deal Value
- Deal value rose to **~$80 billion** (from $55B in 2024); volume up **~20%** to **130+ deals**
- Accounts for **~30% of overall deal volume** and **≥22% of deal value** each year since 2020
- **Europe:** ~40% increase in volume, ~70% increase in value vs. 2024
- **North America:** Value up 20%, volume flat vs. 2024
- Investor themes: CDMOs, SMOs, generics, consumer health, animal health (less exposed to payer pressures)

**Notable Exits:**
- Clario (Nordic Capital/Astorg) sold to Thermo Fisher Scientific for **~$9 billion**
- Dotmatics (Insight Partners) sold to Siemens for **>$5 billion**

### Provider — Spurred by Services & IT
- Deal value **+57%** to an estimated **$62 billion**
- **Healthcare IT within provider doubled to $32 billion**
- Investors focused on technology-enabled assets: analytics, workforce optimization, platform solutions
- Trends: site-of-care migration, retail healthcare M&A (dental, veterinary), lab/diagnostic platforms, workforce solutions

**Notable Deal:** Warburg Pincus's sale of ModMed to Clearlake Capital (valuation **>$5 billion**)

### Medtech — Leading Post-COVID Growth
- Deal value **nearly doubled** to **~$33 billion**; volume up **~20%** to **~88 deals**
- Investors applying proven playbooks: revenue growth, margin expansion, multiple expansion
- Focus on large-scale take-privates and carve-outs from public markets

**Major Deals:**
- **Blackstone & TPG → Hologic** (take-private, **~$18.3 billion**; ~9% of total 2025 healthcare PE deal value)
- **Carlyle Group → Vantive** (renal care carve-out from Baxter, closed early 2025)

**Notable IPO (excluded from exit stats):** Medline raised **>$6 billion** at valuation **>$50 billion** (acquired in 2021 for ~$34B by Blackstone, Carlyle, Hellman & Friedman consortium)

> "Medtech is gaining momentum as investors see opportunities to deploy proven value-creation playbooks: focusing on revenue growth, margin expansion, and multiple expansion, while managing downside risk."

---

## 🔄 Deal Type Trends

### Sponsor-to-Sponsor Deals — Record Surge
- **Record highs** in both volume and value
- **>150 deals** with **>$120 billion** in estimated value
- **>30 deals exceeded $1 billion** (vs. just 8 in 2024)
- Public-to-private and carve-out transactions also growing on absolute basis since 2023

### High-Value Activity Driving Average Deal Size
- Growth in deals **>$1 billion** is outsized relative to volume
- Deals **<$1 billion** saw volume up **56%** but remain well below 2021 highs
- Reflects investor

title: What's Driving Healthcare PE in 2026? Regulation, Value Creation ...
content:
# Summary: What's Driving Healthcare PE in 2026? Regulation, Value Creation, and the New Deal Playbook

**Source:** The Healthcare Investor (McGuireWoods LLP)  
**Subject:** An overview of a podcast interview featuring **Holly Buckley**, Chair of Health Care at McGuireWoods, discussing her outlook on the healthcare private equity market in 2026. 

---

## 🎙️ Podcast & Event Details
*   **Program:** The Healthcare Success Podcast
*   **Featured Speaker:** Holly Buckley (Chair of McGuireWoods’ Healthcare Department)
*   **Upcoming Event:** McGuireWoods annual **Healthcare Private Equity and Finance Conference** 
    *   *Location:* Chicago
    *   *Dates:* April 29-30

## 🔑 Key Discussion Points: The 2026 Healthcare PE Outlook
The podcast interview covers several critical shifts and focus areas for healthcare private equity in the coming year:

*   **Strategic Shifts:** *"Deal discipline has replaced the rapid roll-up strategies of prior years."*
*   **Sector Interest:** Analysis of which specific healthcare sectors are currently drawing the highest level of investor interest.
*   **Regulatory Complexity:** How growing regulatory hurdles at both the **state and federal levels** are actively affecting transaction timelines and increasing deal costs.
*   **Platform Differentiation:** Key factors that separate high-value platforms from the rest of the market.
*   **Artificial Intelligence:** Evaluating which AI developments are worth taking seriously for PE investment versus those that are not yet ready.

## 👤 Speaker Profile: Holly Buckley
*   **Role:** Chair of McGuireWoods’ Healthcare Department (one of the largest healthcare practices in the U.S.).
*   **Expertise:** A national leader in healthcare M&A, private equity, and finance transactions.
*   **Client Base:** PE funds, hospitals, health systems, medical practices, physician-owned hospitals, and ambulatory surgery centers.
*   **Specialties:** Structuring transactions, regulatory due diligence, affiliation agreements, and compliance matters.

## 🏢 About McGuireWoods & The Healthcare Investor
*   **Firm Scale:** McGuireWoods employs over 1,000 lawyers across 21 strategically located offices worldwide.
*   **Target Industries:** Automotive, energy resources, healthcare, technology, and transportation.
*   **Blog Editors:** The Healthcare Investor is edited by firm partners Amber McGraw Walsh, Geoff Cockrell, Holly Buckley, Trey Andrews, and Kayla McCann Marty.

title: [PDF] Private Capital's Evolving Playbook for Healthcare and Life Sciences
content:
# Private Capital's Evolving Playbook for Healthcare and Life Sciences
*Source: Latham & Watkins Analysis | Authors: Christoph W.G. Engeler, Robbie McLaren, Melanie Howard, Henning C. Schneider, Jacob Fontaine, Samantha Peacock*

---

## 📊 Market Performance & Deal Flow (2019-2025)

**Global Healthcare PE Deal Value** (Excluding Add-Ons):
- **2024**: $103B (third-highest year on record)
- **2025 YTD** (as of Dec): $149.8B
- Peak year: **2021** at $284.4B

**Biotech Adjacencies Investment**:
- **2025 YTD**: $35.7B (significant surge from $5.0B in 2024)
- Includes: pharma supply chain, storage, distribution, and delivery

**Rare Disease Therapeutics Investment**:
- **2025 YTD**: $8.3B
- Steady growth from $6.2B in 2024

**Regional Distribution** (Dec 2025 YTD):
- **Americas**: 50.9% ($54.8B)
- **EMEA**: 36.6% ($18.7B)
- **APAC**: 12.5%

---

## 🔑 Key Takeaways

> - Continuation vehicles have become a popular mechanism for providing liquidity in a tight exit market.
> - Investors are looking for real-world, measurable AI use cases, with a focus on drug discovery and optimizing the journey from molecule to market.
> - Biotech adjacencies and rare disease therapeutics offer strong pricing power, regulatory incentives, and scalable platforms.

---

## 🔄 Evolving Investment Landscape

**Key Shift**: Capital flowing into healthcare **earlier**, targeting **younger companies** with built-in platform potential.

**Traditional LBO model being challenged** by:
- More complex structures
- Value creation hinging on sophisticated levers
- AI integration requirements
- Strategic resilience over longer development timelines

### Market Dynamics (Buyer's Market)

> "It is a buyer's market: Investors are pushing hard on valuations, waiting for more data, and applying greater scrutiny to projections, ARR, and EBITDA."

**Characteristics**:
- Persistent gap between buyer and seller valuation expectations
- Only most compelling companies achieve sought valuations
- Alternative paths for others: structured financings, liquidation preferences, licensing transactions, deferred commitments

**Regulatory Impact**:
- New US regulatory proposals (most favored nation drug-pricing regime)
- Concerns about disrupted global supply chains and drug shortages (especially Europe)
- Stalled exits and withdrawn deals
- Investors **doubling down on European opportunities** for geographic diversification

---

## 🏗️ Deal Structuring Innovations

**Tools Bridging Valuation Gaps**:
- Structured deals tied to **sales and regulatory milestones**
- **Earnouts**
- Enhanced governance provisions
- Joint development committees
- Optionality in exit pathways

---

## 🚀 Continuation Vehicles (CVs)

> "Continuation vehicles are an increasingly popular feature of the healthcare and life sciences landscape."

**CV Market Data**:
- **68 healthcare/life sciences CVs** closed globally since 2022
- Combined value: **>$29 billion**
- **2025 on track to be busiest year for healthcare CVs on record**

**CV Features**:
- Allow GPs to continue managing high-performing assets longer
- Give LPs option to stay invested or crystallize returns
- Enable asset bundling, selective strip sales, stapled deals

**Success Factors**: Alignment, common ground on key issues, liquidity horizons, satisfactory buyer control levels

---

## 🤖 AI Integration Opportunities

> "Investors are looking for real-world, measurable AI use cases, with a focus on drug discovery and optimizing the journey from molecule to market."

**AI Applications**:
- **Patient recruitment algorithms** → slash trial timelines
- **Deep-learning diagnostics** → boost clinical accuracy
- **Automated regulatory submissions**

**Winner Profile**: Companies embedding AI into operating systems, leveraging data **from discovery to delivery**

---

## 💊 Biotech Adjacencies & Rare Disease Therapeutics

### Biotech Adjacencies
**Examples**: Pharma supply-chain services, GLP-1 autoinjectors, biologics fill-finish technologies

**Advantages**:
- Strong pricing power
- Differentiated exposure to innovation cycles
- **Avoid binary risks** of new molecule development

### Rare Disease Therapeutics
**Drivers of Interest**:
- Lean, innovation-driven models
- Government incentives and regulatory accelerants
- Orphan drug exclusivity
- Fast-track approvals and premium pricing
- Pharma companies welcome external funding to offset high R&D costs

### 🇪🇺 EU Pharmaceutical Legislation Update (December 2025)
- **Orphan medicines**: 9 years of market exclusivity
- **+2 additional years** for addressing unmet medical needs
- **Transferable exclusivity voucher** for antimicrobials (+1 year market protection)

**Investor Benefits**: Highly defensible revenue streams, regulatory-driven barriers to entry, scalable platforms combining predictable cash flows with innovation exposure

---

## 🎯 Platform Thinking & Scalability

**Investor Focus**:
- Scalable infrastructure
- Repeatable processes
- Systems supporting multiple revenue streams
- Scie

title: Private Equity's Dental Playbook - Decisions in Dentistry
content:
# Summary: Private Equity’s Dental Playbook

**Source:** [Decisions in Dentistry](https://decisionsindentistry.com/2026/03/private-equitys-dental-playbook-2/)  
**Author:** Kristen Pratt Machado  
**Topic:** An analysis by Tony Lo Sasso, PhD, published in *Forbes*, examining forthcoming research on the real-world impact of private equity (PE) acquisitions in dentistry. 

## Executive Overview
Private equity's role in dentistry is highly polarizing. However, new research highlighted by Tony Lo Sasso, PhD, reveals that the impact of PE acquisitions is more nuanced than typical headlines suggest. While PE firms are not using market power to extract higher reimbursement rates from insurers, they are fundamentally altering clinical operations by shifting the types of procedures performed to boost revenue.

## Key Findings from the Research

**1. Insurer Reimbursements Remained Flat**
*   Contrary to popular belief, PE acquisition was **not** associated with higher negotiated reimbursement rates from insurers. 
*   This indicates that PE-backed groups are not simply leveraging market power to force better contract terms.

**2. Shift in Care and Pricing**
*   *What did change:* Acquired practices increased their list prices and shifted clinical care away from lower-cost preventive services toward more profitable restorative procedures.
*   *The mechanism:* Revenue growth is driven by altering the mix and intensity of care, rather than getting paid more per procedure.

> *"Revenue growth did not seem to come from insurers paying more per procedure, but from changing the mix and intensity of care being delivered."*

**3. Medicaid Participation Was Unaffected**
*   The study found no evidence that PE ownership reduced Medicaid participation. 
*   This counters the common fear that financially driven practice models inherently reduce access to care for lower-income populations.

## The Long-Term Threat: Market Consolidation
While the immediate data doesn't show price gouging, Lo Sasso warns that the true long-term issue is **competition**.
*   If industry consolidation continues, local markets will become highly concentrated.
*   This shift could eventually hand larger groups massive bargaining leverage over insurers.
*   **Bottom line:** Today's operational shifts (favoring high-margin procedures over preventive care) could easily evolve into tomorrow's pricing crisis.

## Key Takeaway for Dental Professionals
The research concludes that while private equity is not a "simple villain," it is far from a neutral player. The most pressing immediate threat is the subtle reshaping of treatment patterns, where financial incentives pressure practices to prioritize high-margin restorative work over foundational preventive care.

> *"The real threat may emerge when consolidation weakens competition and changes begin to influence not just business operations, but the kind of care patients receive."*

***
*Note: The original Forbes article by Tony Lo Sasso can be found [here](https://www.forbes.com/sites/anthonylosasso/2026/03/04/private-equity-isnt-the-real-issue-in-healthcare-competition-is/).*
